key_insight: A MACE machine-learned interatomic potential captures molten Li2CO3 structure and transport more accurately than NequIP, revealing concerted lithium diffusion and a temperature-driven shift from anisotropic to isotropic motion.
engineering_relevance: The model enables scalable prediction of molten carbonate properties relevant to fuel cells, carbon capture, and battery electrolytes, reducing reliance on costly ab initio molecular dynamics.
method: Trained MACE and NequIP on melt-quench ab initio molecular dynamics data.
Benchmarked transferability and force/energy accuracy of both equivariant graph potentials.
Used the optimized MACE model for large-scale molecular dynamics to analyze structure, viscosity, and ionic transport.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that MACE better reproduces experimentally measured static structure factors and shear viscosity, with Haven’s ratios of 0.20–0.40 indicating strong concerted Li motion.
watch_item: Performance is tied to the training regime and composition/temperature range of the melt-quench data, so extrapolation beyond those conditions may be unreliable.
paper_title: Probing Structure and Ionic Transport in Molten Lithium Carbonate
